segments.title: North African forces depended on Mediterranean sailings
segments.summary: The Duisburg convoy was one of the regular Mediterranean sailings that sustained German and Italian forces in North Africa.
segments.body: Clarke presents the Duisburg convoy as part of a recurring Axis supply route rather than an isolated voyage. The regular Mediterranean sailings sustained both German and Italian forces in North Africa, and he cautions against treating the campaign as though only the Germans required this traffic. The armies ashore depended on the supplies carried by sea, making the convoy route an operational lifeline. Destroying one sailing therefore affected the land campaign as well as the merchant ships caught in the action.

evidence.note: The transcript describes regular German and Italian sailings to resupply forces in North Africa.

segments.title: Codebreaking and Malta reconnaissance expose the convoy
segments.summary: Enigma and Italian codebreaking identified the sailing, while RAF reconnaissance helped locate it and distorted Axis estimates of Malta's strength.
segments.body: British intelligence learned of the regular sailing through both Enigma and Italian codebreaking, giving two sources of confirmation. An RAF reconnaissance aircraft was then sent to locate the convoy visibly, providing an apparent explanation for how it had been found. This helped conceal the deeper communications-security failure from the Axis. Clarke adds that repeated successful sightings led Axis forces to overestimate Malta's reconnaissance strength and, by extension, the size of its garrison, showing how signals intelligence could distort enemy assessments as well as guide an interception.

evidence.note: The transcript links codebreaking, RAF reconnaissance, and Axis overestimation of Malta's reconnaissance capacity.

segments.title: Aurora, Penelope, and Upholder strike the convoy
segments.summary: Aurora and Penelope, with two destroyers and the Malta-based submarine Upholder, destroyed the convoy and sank about 40,000 tons of shipping.
segments.body: The Arethusa-class light cruisers Aurora and Penelope sailed with two destroyers to intercept the convoy. Clarke describes the surface force as wiping out the entire convoy in one action. He estimates that about 40,000 tons of shipping, spread across multiple ships, was sunk. The Malta-based submarine Upholder also joined the attack, placing a U-class boat alongside the cruisers and destroyers in the destruction of the supply sailing.

evidence.note: The transcript names Aurora, Penelope, their destroyers, the 40,000-ton loss, and Upholder's Malta base.
